summary: The author placed 20th by adapting the Caformer architecture for seismic inversion, modifying it to accept 5 input planes side-by-side as a single-channel 1000x350 image. Key techniques included physics-based velocity scaling augmentation applied during training and test-time augmentation (TTA) via median aggregation. The author also implemented a semi-supervised self-training loop using forward modeling to generate synthetic seismic data from test predictions, combined with deep supervision using a 10-class linear head on the middle UNet feature.
extensive_summary:
## Model Architecture & Input Modification
The solution builds on the Caformer architecture, originally shared by another competitor. The author modified the input layer to process 5 input planes side-by-side as a single-channel 1000x350 image. Deep supervision was implemented by attaching a linear head to the middle feature map of the UNet backbone (9x9 size) to predict 10 classes, which significantly improved performance.

## Data Augmentation & TTA
A physics-inspired velocity scaling technique was used for augmentation. Multiplying velocity by alpha > 1 shrinks the seismic image along the time dimension (padded with zeros), while alpha < 1 expands it (truncated to original size). During training, alpha was randomly sampled from [0.8, 1.2]. For TTA, outputs were computed across 10 alpha values and aggregated via median.

## Forward Modeling & Self-Training
Leveraging external forward modeling code, the author generated synthetic seismic data from velocity predictions. Backpropagation through the forward model proved too slow, so a semi-supervised approach was adopted: test predictions were augmented, converted to seismic data, and used as input to predict a second velocity estimate. An in-batch strategy added a test batch every 6 training batches, backpropagating the MAE between the original and re-predicted velocities. This approach was also run exclusively on test data near the deadline.

## Validation & Lessons Learned
The validation strategy relied on 20 files from a public notebook, but the CV score stopped correlating with the LB around a CV of 12. Attempts to use other backbones failed to converge, and a mixture-of-experts routing strategy improved CV but not LB despite high routing accuracy. Frequency/location-based data generation improved LB by 1 but was computationally prohibitive.
competition_flow: Raw seismic/velocity data is processed through a modified Caformer model with physics-based velocity scaling augmentation, augmented via forward modeling from test predictions, supervised with a 10-class linear head, and aggregated via median TTA for submission.
data_processing: Velocity scaling augmentation (shrink/expand/pad/truncate based on alpha)
Padding with zeros and truncation to original size
TTA with 10 alpha values taking median
Forward modeling to generate seismic data from velocity predictions
cv_strategy: Validation on 20 files from a public notebook; CV-LB correlation broke down around CV=12.
ensembling: Test-time augmentation with 10 velocity scaling alpha values, aggregating outputs via median.
insights: Physics-based velocity scaling is highly effective for both training and TTA.
Generating synthetic data from test predictions and retraining on it significantly boosts performance.
Deep supervision with a linear head on the middle UNet feature provides a substantial performance gain.
critical_findings: The validation score stopped correlating with the leaderboard around a CV of 12.
A mixture-of-experts routing strategy improved CV but failed to improve LB despite 99.55% routing accuracy.
Frequency/location-based data generation improved LB by 1 but was too slow to be practical.
what_did_not_work: Training other backbones did not converge sufficiently.
Mixture-of-experts routing improved CV but not LB.
Generating data using frequency and location moves took too long and only yielded a minor LB improvement.
